questions.question: Define a process and explain its key characteristics.
keyPoints: A process is a program in execution
It is an active entity with its own resources, state, and memory
Key characteristics: unique PID, own address space, execution context, and system resources
Process goes through various states during its lifetime

questions.question: Explain the five basic states of a process with a state transition diagram.
keyPoints: NEW: Process being created but not yet admitted to ready queue
READY: Process has all resources, waiting for CPU allocation
RUNNING: Process instructions being executed on CPU
WAITING: Process blocked waiting for I/O or event
TERMINATED: Process completed execution
Transitions: New→Ready, Ready→Running, Running→Ready, Running→Waiting, Waiting→Ready, Running→Terminated

questions.question: What is a Process Control Block (PCB)? List its main components.
keyPoints: PCB is a kernel data structure that stores all information about a process
Components: Process ID (PID), Process State, Program Counter, CPU Registers
Memory Management: page tables, segment tables, memory limits
I/O Status: open files, pending I/O requests
Accounting: CPU time used, resource usage
Scheduling: priority, queue pointers

questions.question: Differentiate between a zombie process and an orphan process.
keyPoints: Zombie: Terminated process whose parent hasn't called wait() to read exit status
Zombie remains in process table consuming minimal resources
Orphan: Running process whose parent has terminated
Orphan is adopted by init process (PID 1) which performs wait()
Both are process states resulting from parent-child relationship issues

questions.question: Explain the fork() and exec() system calls with their purposes.
keyPoints: fork(): Creates a new process (child) by duplicating parent process
Returns child's PID to parent, 0 to child, -1 on error
Child gets copy of parent's address space
exec(): Replaces current process image with new program
PID remains same but code, data, heap, stack are replaced
fork()+exec() together create new program execution

questions.question: Describe the memory layout of a process.
keyPoints: Text Section: Contains executable code (read-only)
Data Section: Initialized and uninitialized global/static variables (BSS)
Heap: Dynamic memory allocation during runtime (grows upward)
Stack: Function calls, local variables, return addresses (grows downward)
Each process has its own private address space

questions.question: What is context switching? Explain its role in process management.
keyPoints: Context switching is saving the state of currently running process and loading another process's state
CPU registers, program counter, and process state are saved to PCB
New process state is loaded from its PCB to CPU registers
Enables multiple processes to share single CPU
Overhead: purely computational work, reduces system efficiency

questions.question: Explain the process creation mechanism in operating systems. Discuss fork() system call, how child process gets its own address space, and how exec() is used to run different programs.
keyPoints: Process creation involves: allocating PCB, assigning unique PID, allocating memory, copying context
fork() creates child by copying parent's address space (copy-on-write optimization)
Parent and child execute concurrently after fork()
Parent gets child's PID, child gets 0 as return value
exec() family replaces process image: text, data, heap, stack with new program
Common pattern: fork() to create child, then child calls exec() to run new program
wait() allows parent to synchronize with child's termination
Exit status collected to prevent zombie processes

questions.question: Draw and explain the process state diagram. Include all possible state transitions and explain when each transition occurs.
keyPoints: Five states: NEW, READY, RUNNING, WAITING, TERMINATED
NEW→READY: OS admits process after initialization
READY→RUNNING: CPU scheduler dispatches process
RUNNING→READY: Time slice expired or preemptive scheduling
RUNNING→WAITING: Process waits for I/O or event
WAITING→READY: I/O completed or event occurred
RUNNING→TERMINATED: Normal exit or fatal error
READY→TERMINATED (rare): Parent terminates child
WAITING→TERMINATED: Parent terminates child waiting

questions.question: Explain the difference between program and process. Also discuss CPU-bound and I/O-bound processes with examples.
keyPoints: Program: Passive entity (file on disk) containing instructions
Process: Active entity (program in execution) with resources and state
Process has: PID, memory, registers, files, children
Multiple processes can run same program simultaneously
CPU-bound: Spends most time executing (computations)
Examples: Scientific calculations, video rendering, compiler
I/O-bound: Most time waiting for I/O operations
Examples: Text editor, web browser, file operations
Scheduling affects performance: CPU-bound needs longer time slices
Modern systems use mixed approach for fairness
